draft.introduction: Nagarjuna (c. 150–250 CE) is the most important philosopher in the Mahayana Buddhist tradition. His Mulamadhyamakakarika (Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way) systematically demolishes every metaphysical category that humans use to make sense of reality: selfhood, causation, time, motion, change. Chapter 18 turns this analysis on the self (atman) and asks whether there is anything permanent, substantial, or essential in the human person that could survive death, achieve liberation, or—in the terms of the theosis debate—be divinized. Nagarjuna’s answer is devastating: there is no self. If the self were identical with the aggregates (the five components of experience: form, sensation, perception, mental formations, consciousness), it would arise and cease with them. If the self were different from the aggregates, it would have no characteristics at all. Either way, there is nothing there to be deified. The Buddhist critique cuts deeper than any monotheist objection to theosis, because it attacks not just the possibility of union with God but the very existence of the entity that would be united.

translation.text: If the self were the same as the aggregates, it would be subject to arising and ceasing. If the self were different from the aggregates, it would have the characteristics of non-aggregates.
translation.original_text: आत्मा स्कन्धा यदि स्यादुदयव्ययभाग् भवेत् । स्कन्धेभ्यो यदि स्यादस्कन्धलक्षणम् भवेत् ।।

translation.text: If there is no self, how can there be anything belonging to the self? The cessation of self and what belongs to self brings about the relinquishment of “I” and “mine.”
translation.original_text: आत्मन्येव न चेत् कुत आत्मीयं भविष्यति । निरात्मनिरात्मीयादहङ्कारममार्गतः ।।

translation.text: Karma and afflictions cease through their cessation; karma and afflictions arise from conceptual construction. Conceptual construction arises from mental fabrication. Fabrication ceases in emptiness.
translation.original_text: कर्मक्लेशक्षयान्मोक्षः कर्मक्लेशा विकल्पतः । ते प्रपञ्चात् प्रपञ्चस्तु शून्यतायां निरुध्यते ।।

translation.text: It has been taught that the self exists and that non-self exists. But the Buddhas have also taught that neither self nor non-self exists.
translation.original_text: आत्मेत्यपि प्रज्ञप्तमनात्मेत्यपि देशितम् । नात्मा नानात्मेत्यपि देशितं बुद्धैर्महात्मभिः ।।

translator_notes.note: The dilemma Nagarjuna constructs is exhaustive. Either the self is the aggregates or it is not. If it is, then it is impermanent (since the aggregates constantly arise and cease) and therefore cannot be an eternal soul capable of deification. If it is not, then it has no observable characteristics and is a metaphysical fiction. Both Hindu atman theology and Christian soul theology depend on a substantial self that persists through change; Nagarjuna demolishes the philosophical basis for both.

The term shunyata (शून्यता, emptiness) does not mean nothingness. It means that all phenomena lack inherent, independent existence—they exist only in dependence on causes, conditions, and conceptual designation. This is the Middle Way between eternalism (things have permanent essence) and nihilism (nothing exists at all). For the theosis debate, emptiness undermines not just the self that would be deified but the God with whom it would be united. If all things are empty of inherent existence, then “becoming God” is a category error built on two illusions.
